questions.text: Which one of the following is the correct matching of the events occurring during the menstrual cycle?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Look out for the catch, endometrium regenerates and not the myometrium.

The secretory phase is also called as luteal phase. The luteinizing hormone or LH is secreted by the anterior lobe of the pituitary gland. LH causes ovulation. LH stimulates cells of ovarian follicles to develop the corpus luteum. Corpus luteum secretes large amount of progesterone.
Look out for the catch, endometrium breaks down and not the myometrium.
There is no fall in the secretion of progesterone. Progesterone is secreted by the corpus leteum. It forms after ovulation.

questions.explanation: a) Proliferative phase:The proliferative phase is the first phase of the menstrual cycle in females. It occurs after menstruation when the uterine lining (endometrium) has been shed. During this phase, under the influence of estrogen produced by the developing ovarian follicles, the endometrium begins to thicken and regenerate. The proliferative phase is characterized by the growth and proliferation of endometrial cells and the development of blood vessels in preparation for a potential pregnancy. It typically occurs from days 6 to 14 of a 28-day menstrual cycle.b) Secretory phase:The secretory phase is the second phase of the menstrual cycle. It occurs after ovulation when an egg is released from the ovary. During this phase, the ruptured follicle in the ovary transforms into the corpus luteum, which produces progesterone. Progesterone prepares the endometrium for possible implantation of a fertilized egg. Under the influence of progesterone, the endometrial glands secrete a nutrient-rich fluid to support the developing embryo. If pregnancy does not occur, the corpus luteum will regress, progesterone levels will drop, and the secretory phase will transition into the proliferative phase again. The secretory phase typically occurs from days 15 to 28 of a 28-day menstrual cycle.c) Menstruation:Menstruation is a natural process that occurs in females during their reproductive years. It is the shedding of the uterine lining (endometrium) when a fertilized egg does not implant in the uterus. Menstruation marks the beginning of a new menstrual cycle and is characterized by the discharge of blood, mucus, and tissue from the vagina. It typically lasts for 3 to 7 days but can vary from woman to woman. The shedding of the endometrium is driven by a decrease in estrogen and progesterone levels when there is no pregnancy.d) Ovulation:Ovulation is a critical event in the menstrual cycle. It is the release of a mature egg (ovum) from the ovary into the fallopian tube, where it can potentially be fertilized by sperm. Ovulation typically occurs around the midpoint of a woman's menstrual cycle, approximately 14 days before the start of the next menstrual period. Ovulation is triggered by a surge in luteinizing hormone (LH) from the pituitary gland, which stimulates the release of the egg from the dominant ovarian follicle. The released egg is then available for fertilization for about 24 hours. If fertilization does not occur, the egg will be absorbed by the body, and the menstrual cycle will continue with the shedding of the uterine lining during menstruation.

questions.text: Fetal ejection reflex in human female is induced by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While oxytocin plays a crucial role in childbirth (parturition) by stimulating uterine contractions during labor, it is not directly responsible for the fetal ejection reflex. Oxytocin is released from the maternal pituitary gland and helps facilitate the progression of labor and delivery of the baby, but it is not the primary trigger for the fetal ejection reflex.

This is the correct option. The fetal ejection reflex, also known as the Ferguson reflex, is a natural response triggered by the presence of a fully developed fetus and placenta. As the fetus reaches full term and is ready for delivery, certain signals from the fully developed fetus and placenta activate the maternal body's natural reflexes, leading to a strong urge to push during the second stage of labor. This reflex helps facilitate the delivery of the baby through the birth canal and into the world.

The differentiation of mammary glands, which involves changes in breast tissue to prepare for lactation, is not directly related to the fetal ejection reflex. While lactation is a critical process after childbirth, it is not the trigger for the fetal ejection reflex during labor and delivery.

The pressure exerted by amniotic fluid is not the primary cause of the fetal ejection reflex. The amniotic fluid serves as a protective cushion for the developing fetus during pregnancy but is not directly involved in the mechanism of the fetal ejection reflex during childbirth.

questions.explanation: Note: Parturition is induced by a complex neuroendocrine mechanism. The signals for parturition originate from the fully developed foetus and the placenta which induce mild uterine contractions called foetal ejection reflex. This triggers release of oxytocin from the maternal pituitary. Oxytocin acts on the uterine muscle and causes stronger uterine contractions, which in turn stimulates further secretion of oxytocin. The stimulatory reflex between the uterine contraction and oxytocin secretion continues resulting in stronger and stronger contractions. This leads to expulsion of the baby out of the uterus through the birth canal.The correct option is: b) Fully developed foetus and placenta.Explanation of each option:a) Release of oxytocin from pituitary:While oxytocin plays a crucial role in childbirth (parturition) by stimulating uterine contractions during labor, it is not directly responsible for the foetal ejection reflex. Oxytocin is released from the maternal pituitary gland and helps facilitate the progression of labor and delivery of the baby, but it is not the primary trigger for the foetal ejection reflex.b) Fully developed foetus and placenta:This is the correct option. The foetal ejection reflex, also known as the Ferguson reflex, is a natural response triggered by the presence of a fully developed foetus and placenta. As the foetus reaches full term and is ready for delivery, certain signals from the fully developed foetus and placenta activate the maternal body's natural reflexes, leading to a strong urge to push during the second stage of labor. This reflex helps facilitate the delivery of the baby through the birth canal and into the world.c) Differentiation of mammary glands:The differentiation of mammary glands, which involves changes in breast tissue to prepare for lactation, is not directly related to the foetal ejection reflex. While lactation is a critical process after childbirth, it is not the trigger for the foetal ejection reflex during labor and delivery.d) Pressure exerted by amniotic fluid:The pressure exerted by amniotic fluid is not the primary cause of the foetal ejection reflex. The amniotic fluid serves as a protective cushion for the developing foetus during pregnancy but is not directly involved in the mechanism of the foetal ejection reflex during childbirth.In summary, option (b) Fully developed foetus and placenta is correct. The foetal ejection reflex is induced by the presence of a fully developed foetus and placenta, leading to a natural urge to push during labor and facilitate the delivery of the baby.

questions.text: Which one of the following is the most likely root cause of why menstruation is not taking place in regularly cycling human females?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Maintenance of the hypertrophical endometrial lining
options.explanation: This option suggests that the thickening of the endometrial lining would inhibit menstruation. However, a thickened endometrium is typically a response to hormonal signals rather than a root cause of absent menstruation.

options.text: Maintenance of high concentration of sex hormones in the bloodstream
options.explanation: While high levels of sex hormones, particularly progesterone, can prevent menstruation, they are often a result of pregnancy or hormonal treatments rather than a direct cause of irregular cycles.

The corpus luteum secretes hormones that maintain the endometrium. Retention of a well-developed corpus luteum is generally due to fertilization and can explain the absence of menstruation, but it is not the primary reason for all cases of missed periods.

This is the correct answer. When fertilization occurs, the hormonal changes prevent the shedding of the endometrial lining, leading to the absence of menstruation. This indicates the body is preparing for pregnancy.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Fertilisation of the ovum, as this process leads to increased levels of hormones such as human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG), which prevent the normal menstrual cycle from occurring. The other options, while related to hormonal regulation and the reproductive cycle, do not directly address the root cause of missed periods in the context of regular cycles. Maintenance of the hypertrophical endometrial lining is a consequence of hormonal signals, not a direct cause. Maintenance of high concentration of sex hormones might occur due to various factors, including pregnancy, but is also not a direct cause of missed cycles. Retention of a well-developed corpus luteum is typically a sign of pregnancy as well, rather than a standalone cause for all cases of absent menstruation.

questions.text: In humans, at the end of the first meiotic division, the male germ cells differentiate into the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Spermatids are the result of the second meiotic division of male germ cells. After the first meiotic division, primary spermatocytes are formed. These primary spermatocytes then undergo the second meiotic division to produce haploid spermatids. Spermatids are the immediate precursors of spermatozoa (sperms) and further undergo spermiogenesis, a process of cellular differentiation and morphological changes, to develop into mature sperm cells.

Spermatogonia are the undifferentiated diploid stem cells that reside in the seminiferous tubules of the testes. They are the "germ cells" that continually divide by mitosis to replenish the pool of germ cells and provide a continuous supply of cells for spermatogenesis. Spermatogonia differentiate into primary spermatocytes when they enter meiosis.

Primary spermatocytes are formed when spermatogonia enter meiosis (the process of gamete formation involving two rounds of cell division). These primary spermatocytes are diploid cells, meaning they contain the full complement of chromosomes (46 chromosomes in humans). They undergo the first meiotic division to form secondary spermatocytes.

This is the correct option. At the end of the first meiotic division, primary spermatocytes divide into secondary spermatocytes. This division is reductional, meaning the number of chromosomes is reduced by half. Secondary spermatocytes are haploid cells with 23 chromosomes each in humans. After this division, secondary spermatocytes will undergo the second meiotic division to form spermatids, which will eventually mature into spermatozoa (sperms).

questions.explanation: Note: During embryonic development the primordial germ cells migrate to the testis where they become spermatogonia. At puberty the spermatogonia proliferate rapidly by mitosis. Some undergo growth phase to become primary spermatocytes that further undergo through meiotic division I to become secondary spermatocytes. After completion of meiotic division II the secondary spermatocytes produce spermatids which differentiate to form spermatozoa.The correct option is: d) Secondary spermatocytes.Explanation of each option:a) Spermatids:Spermatids are the result of the second meiotic division of male germ cells. After the first meiotic division, primary spermatocytes are formed. These primary spermatocytes then undergo the second meiotic division to produce haploid spermatids. Spermatids are the immediate precursors of spermatozoa (sperms) and further undergo spermiogenesis, a process of cellular differentiation and morphological changes, to develop into mature sperm cells.b) Spermatogonia:Spermatogonia are the undifferentiated diploid stem cells that reside in the seminiferous tubules of the testes. They are the "germ cells" that continually divide by mitosis to replenish the pool of germ cells and provide a continuous supply of cells for spermatogenesis. Spermatogonia differentiate into primary spermatocytes when they enter meiosis.c) Primary spermatocytes:Primary spermatocytes are formed when spermatogonia enter meiosis (the process of gamete formation involving two rounds of cell division). These primary spermatocytes are diploid cells, meaning they contain the full complement of chromosomes (46 chromosomes in humans). They undergo the first meiotic division to form secondary spermatocytes.d) Secondary spermatocytes:This is the correct option. At the end of the first meiotic division, primary spermatocytes divide into secondary spermatocytes. This division is a reductional division, meaning the number of chromosomes is reduced by half. Secondary spermatocytes are haploid cells with 23 chromosomes each in humans. After this division, secondary spermatocytes will undergo the second meiotic division to form spermatids, which will eventually mature into spermatozoa (sperms).In summary, option (d) Secondary spermatocytes is the correct answer. At the end of the first meiotic division, male germ cells differentiate into secondary spermatocytes, which are haploid cells with 23 chromosomes. The process of meiosis is essential for the generation of haploid sperm cells from diploid spermatogonia.

questions.text: Which one of the following statements is incorrect about menstruation?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: At menopause in the female, there is an especially abrupt decrease in gonadotropic hormones
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Menopause is a natural biological process that occurs in females usually around the age of 45 to 55, marking the end of reproductive years. During menopause, there is a significant decrease in gonadotropic hormones, such as luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), which are normally produced by the pituitary gland and regulate the menstrual cycle. The abrupt decrease in these hormones leads to the cessation of ovulation and menstruation during menopause.

options.text: The beginning of the cycle of menstruation is called menarche
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Menarche refers to the first occurrence of menstruation in females. It typically occurs during adolescence, around the age of 9 to 15 years, and marks the beginning of the reproductive years.

options.text: During normal menstruation about 40 mL blood is lost
options.explanation: This statement is generally correct. The average amount of blood lost during a normal menstrual period is approximately 30 to 40 milliliters (mL). However, this can vary from woman to woman, and some may experience heavier or lighter bleeding. Menstrual blood also contains other fluids and tissues from the uterine lining, not just pure blood.

options.text: The menstrual fluid can easily clot
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Menstrual fluid does not typically clot. Menstrual blood consists of shed endometrial tissue, blood, and cervical mucus, and it is designed to flow out of the body smoothly. The anticoagulant properties of the blood prevent it from clotting easily within the uterus or the vaginal canal. Clotting of menstrual blood may occur in some cases, but it is not a common or normal occurrence during menstruation.

questions.explanation: Note: Menstruation is a process which involves discharge of blood (45 – 100mL), serous fluid, cell debris and mucosal fragments from cast off endometrial lining due to reduce titre of both estrogen and progesterone hormone. Blood clotting does not occur due to presence of fibrinolysin.The correct option is: d) The menstrual fluid can easily clot.Explanation of each option:a) At menopause in the female, there is especially abrupt decrease in gonadotropic hormones:This statement is correct. Menopause is a natural biological process that occurs in females usually around the age of 45 to 55, marking the end of reproductive years. During menopause, there is a significant decrease in gonadotropic hormones, such as luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), which are normally produced by the pituitary gland and regulate the menstrual cycle. The abrupt decrease in these hormones leads to the cessation of ovulation and menstruation during menopause.b) The beginning of the cycle of menstruation is called menarche:This statement is correct. Menarche refers to the first occurrence of menstruation in females. It typically occurs during adolescence, around the age of 9 to 15 years, and marks the beginning of the reproductive years.c) During normal menstruation about 40 mL blood is lost:This statement is generally correct. The average amount of blood lost during a normal menstrual period is approximately 30 to 40 milliliters (mL). However, this can vary from woman to woman, and some may experience heavier or lighter bleeding. Menstrual blood also contains other fluids and tissues from the uterine lining, not just pure blood.d) The menstrual fluid can easily clot:This statement is incorrect. Menstrual fluid does not typically clot. Menstrual blood consists of shed endometrial tissue, blood, and cervical mucus, and it is designed to flow out of the body smoothly. The anticoagulant properties of the blood prevent it from clotting easily within the uterus or the vaginal canal. Clotting of menstrual blood may occur in some cases, but it is not a common or normal occurrence during menstruation.In summary, option (d) The menstrual fluid can easily clot is incorrect. Menstrual fluid does not typically clot due to its anticoagulant properties, and it is designed to flow smoothly out of the body during menstruation.

questions.text: Which part of the ovary in mammals acts as an endocrine gland after ovulation?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The stroma is a supportive and connective tissue framework of the ovary that provides structural support to the various ovarian components. While it contains blood vessels, nerves, and other supporting structures, it does not act as an endocrine gland after ovulation.

The germinal epithelium is the outermost layer of the ovary, and it was historically thought to be the site of egg production. However, it is now understood that the eggs are formed within the ovarian follicles, not the germinal epithelium. The germinal epithelium does not act as an endocrine gland after ovulation.

The vitelline membrane, also known as the zona pellucida, is an important structure surrounding the oocyte (egg) within the Graafian follicle. It plays a critical role in interactions between the oocyte and surrounding cells during fertilization. However, it is not an endocrine gland and does not produce hormones after ovulation.

This is the correct option. The Graafian follicle, also known as the mature ovarian follicle, is a specialized structure within the ovary that contains a fully developed oocyte. During the menstrual cycle, the Graafian follicle grows and matures under the influence of hormones such as follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) and luteinizing hormone (LH). After ovulation, when the mature egg is released from the ovary, the remaining structure of the Graafian follicle changes and transforms into the corpus luteum. The corpus luteum acts as an endocrine gland and secretes hormones, primarily progesterone, which is crucial for preparing and maintaining the uterus for a potential pregnancy.

questions.explanation: The correct option is: d) Graafian follicle.Explanation of each option:a) Stroma:The stroma is a supportive and connective tissue framework of the ovary that provides structural support to the various ovarian components. While it contains blood vessels, nerves, and other supporting structures, it does not act as an endocrine gland after ovulation.b) Germinal epithelium:The germinal epithelium is the outermost layer of the ovary, and it was historically thought to be the site of egg production. However, it is now understood that the eggs are actually formed within the ovarian follicles, not the germinal epithelium. The germinal epithelium does not act as an endocrine gland after ovulation.c) Vitelline membrane:The vitelline membrane, also known as the zona pellucida, is an important structure surrounding the oocyte (egg) within the Graafian follicle. It plays a critical role in interactions between the oocyte and surrounding cells during fertilization. However, it is not an endocrine gland and does not produce hormones after ovulation.d) Graafian follicle:This is the correct option. The Graafian follicle, also known as the mature ovarian follicle, is a specialized structure within the ovary that contains a fully developed oocyte. During the menstrual cycle, the Graafian follicle grows and matures under the influence of hormones such as follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) and luteinizing hormone (LH). After ovulation, when the mature egg is released from the ovary, the remaining structure of the Graafian follicle undergoes changes and transforms into the corpus luteum. The corpus luteum acts as an endocrine gland and secretes hormones, primarily progesterone, which is crucial for preparing and maintaining the uterus for a potential pregnancy.In summary, option (d) Graafian follicle is the correct option. It acts as an endocrine gland after ovulation, producing hormones such as progesterone, to support the early stages of pregnancy in mammals.

questions.text: Sertoli cells are regulated by the pituitary hormone known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Luteinizing hormone (LH) is a pituitary hormone that plays a crucial role in the female menstrual cycle and male reproductive system. In females, LH surge triggers ovulation, the release of a mature egg from the ovary. In males, LH stimulates Leydig cells in the testes to produce testosterone, which is essential for sperm production (spermatogenesis). LH does not directly regulate Sertoli cells.

This is the correct option. Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) is a pituitary hormone that plays a key role in both males and females. In males, FSH stimulates Sertoli cells in the seminiferous tubules of the testes. Sertoli cells are essential for supporting and nourishing developing sperm cells (spermatogonia) during spermatogenesis. FSH promotes the maturation of sperm cells within the seminiferous tubules.

Growth hormone (GH) is a pituitary hormone that is involved in promoting growth and development, regulating metabolism, and stimulating the production of insulin-like growth factors (IGFs). GH does not directly regulate Sertoli cells or have a direct role in the male reproductive system.

Prolactin is a pituitary hormone primarily known for its role in promoting lactation (milk production) in females after childbirth. Prolactin also has various other roles in the body, including regulation of the immune system and metabolism. Prolactin does not directly regulate Sertoli cells or have a specific role in male reproductive physiology.

questions.explanation: The correct option is: b) FSH.Explanation of each option:a) LH (Luteinizing Hormone):Luteinizing hormone (LH) is a pituitary hormone that plays a crucial role in the female menstrual cycle and male reproductive system. In females, LH surge triggers ovulation, the release of a mature egg from the ovary. In males, LH stimulates Leydig cells in the testes to produce testosterone, which is essential for sperm production (spermatogenesis). LH does not directly regulate Sertoli cells.b) FSH (Follicle-Stimulating Hormone):This is the correct option. Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) is a pituitary hormone that plays a key role in both males and females. In males, FSH stimulates Sertoli cells in the seminiferous tubules of the testes. Sertoli cells are essential for supporting and nourishing developing sperm cells (spermatogonia) during spermatogenesis. FSH promotes the maturation of sperm cells within the seminiferous tubules.c) GH (Growth Hormone):Growth hormone (GH) is a pituitary hormone that is involved in promoting growth and development, regulating metabolism, and stimulating the production of insulin-like growth factors (IGFs). GH does not directly regulate Sertoli cells or have a direct role in the male reproductive system.d) Prolactin:Prolactin is a pituitary hormone primarily known for its role in promoting lactation (milk production) in females after childbirth. Prolactin also has various other roles in the body, including regulation of the immune system and metabolism. Prolactin does not directly regulate Sertoli cells or have a specific role in male reproductive physiology.In summary, option (b) FSH is the correct option. Sertoli cells in the testes are regulated by follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), which promotes the maturation and development of sperm cells during spermatogenesis in males.

questions.text: Withdrawal of which of the following hormones is the immediate cause of menstruation?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The correct answer is Progesterone. Menstruation is triggered by the withdrawal of progesterone. During the menstrual cycle, the lining of the uterus (endometrium) becomes thicker in preparation for a potential pregnancy under the influence of estrogen and progesterone. If fertilization does not occur, progesterone levels decrease, leading to a shedding of the uterine lining, which is what is observed as menstruation.

Estrogen is responsible for the growth and maintenance of the uterine lining. While estrogen levels do fluctuate during the menstrual cycle, it is the withdrawal of progesterone that directly triggers menstruation.
FSH is involved in stimulating the growth of follicles in the ovaries and the maturation of eggs. It is not directly responsible for triggering menstruation.
FSH-RH is a hormone that regulates the release of FSH from the pituitary gland. It does not directly cause menstruation.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Progesterone. Menstruation is triggered by the withdrawal of progesterone. During the menstrual cycle, the lining of the uterus (endometrium) becomes thicker in preparation for a potential pregnancy under the influence of estrogen and progesterone. If fertilization does not occur, progesterone levels decrease, leading to a shedding of the uterine lining, which is what is observed as menstruation.

questions.text: If the mammalian ovum fails to get fertilized, which one of the following is unlikely?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: If fertilization does not occur, the corpus luteum (formed from the ruptured follicle after ovulation) will eventually degenerate and transform into the corpus albicans. This is a normal part of the menstrual cycle.

The corpus luteum secretes progesterone, which is important for maintaining the uterine lining during the early stages of pregnancy. If fertilization does not occur, the corpus luteum regresses, leading to a decline in progesterone secretion.

If fertilization does not occur, there is a negative feedback loop that leads to a decrease in estrogen and progesterone levels. Increased estrogen secretion would be unlikely in the absence of fertilization.
If fertilization does not occur, the reproductive cycle typically progresses to the next menstrual cycle, and a new set of primary follicles begins to develop in the ovaries.

questions.explanation: If the mammalian ovum fails to fertilize choices, (a) and (b) are obvious. Since corpus luteum declines so progesterone also decreases rapidly (progesterone is essential for maintenance of pregnancy). Also estrogen continues to cause growth of the endometrium which ultimately becomes thick enough to breakdown and cause menstruation. Hence choice (c) is incorrect as estrogen secretion does not decrease further. Primary follicles continue developing irrespective of ovulatory condition.

questions.text: Ovulation in the human female normally takes place during the menstrual cycle:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The secretory phase is the post-ovulatory phase, lasting from about day 15 to 28. During this time, the hormone progesterone (produced by the corpus luteum after ovulation) makes the uterine lining more receptive to implantation. Since this entire phase occurs after the egg has already been released, ovulation cannot happen in the middle of it.

options.text: Just before the end of the secretory phase
options.explanation: This timeframe is just before menstruation would begin if fertilization does not occur. At this point, hormone levels are declining, and the body is preparing to shed the uterine lining. Ovulation is a hormonally distinct event that happens much earlier in the cycle, about two weeks before this point.

options.text: At the beginning of the proliferative phase
options.explanation: The proliferative phase begins just after menstruation ends, around day 5 or 6. At this early stage, an ovarian follicle is just starting to mature, and estrogen levels are beginning to rise to rebuild the uterine wall. The hormonal conditions, particularly the peak estrogen and subsequent LH surge required for ovulation, have not yet been met.

options.text: At the end of the proliferative phase
options.explanation: Ovulation, the release of a mature egg from the ovary, typically occurs around day 14 of a 28-day cycle. This event is triggered by a sharp surge in Luteinizing Hormone (LH). This timing coincides precisely with the end of the proliferative phase, during which the uterine lining (endometrium) has been thickening in response to rising estrogen. Ovulation effectively ends the proliferative phase and initiates the next stage, the secretory phase.

questions.explanation: Ovulation (the release of secondary oocyte from the graffian follicle) takes place at the end of proliferative phase of menstrual cycle. During this phase, the follicle stimulating hormone (FSH) secreted by the anterior lobe of the pituitary gland stimulates the ovarian follicle to secrete estrogen. Estrogen stimulates the proliferation of the endometrium of the uterine wall. The endometrium becomes thicker by rapid cell multiplication and this is accompanied by an increase of uterine glands and blood vessels. This phase ends when the ovarian follicle ruptures and ovulation occurs and at the same time the production of estrogen stops.

questions.text: The middle piece of the sperm contains:

options.explanation: While proteins are present in the structure of a sperm cell, they are not primarily concentrated in the middle piece. Proteins are essential components of the sperm cell, but the middle piece's main function is to house the mitochondria.

questions.text: In the fertile human female, approximately on which day of the menstrual cycle does ovulation take place?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. In a typical 28-day menstrual cycle, ovulation usually occurs around day 14. Ovulation is the process of releasing a mature egg (ovum) from the ovary, and it is triggered by a surge in luteinizing hormone (LH) from the pituitary gland. Ovulation around day 14 is commonly used as a reference point for calculating the timing of fertility.
Ovulation is more likely to occur before day 18 in a typical 28-day menstrual cycle. Day 18 might be closer to the end of the fertile window.
Day 1 of the menstrual cycle usually corresponds to the first day of menstruation. Ovulation occurs later in the cycle, typically around day 14, and marks the midpoint of the cycle.
Ovulation usually occurs after day 8 in a typical 28-day menstrual cycle. The fertile window, including ovulation, tends to occur around the middle of the cycle.

questions.text: Extrusion of second polar body from egg nucleus occurs:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: After entry of sperm before completion of fertilization
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. After the entry of the sperm into the egg, the egg nucleus undergoes its second meiotic division to form the second polar body. The second polar body is a small cell fragment that does not have the potential to develop further. This process occurs before the completion of fertilization when the sperm's genetic material fuses with the egg's genetic material.
The extrusion of the second polar body occurs before the completion of fertilization, as part of the egg's preparation to fuse with the sperm's genetic material. It's not a post-fertilization event.
The extrusion of the second polar body occurs after the entry of the sperm into the egg. It is part of the changes that happen within the egg once it is fertilized.

options.text: Without any relation of sperm entry
options.explanation: The extrusion of the second polar body is directly related to the process of fertilization. It occurs as a result of the egg's response to the entry of the sperm and the activation of the fertilization process. questions.text: Acrosome reaction in sperm is triggered by:

options.explanation: Capacitation is a process that sperm undergo in the female reproductive tract. It involves changes in the sperm's membrane and its ability to interact with the egg. The acrosome reaction is a separate process that occurs when the sperm reaches the vicinity of the egg.

The release of these enzymes from the acrosome is indeed a key trigger for the acrosome reaction. The acrosome reaction allows these enzymes to be released, enabling the sperm to penetrate the egg's protective layers.

The influx of sodium ions (Na+) is not typically the primary trigger for the acrosome reaction. While ion fluxes play a role in sperm activation and mobility, they are not the main factor initiating the acrosome reaction.

This results in four spermatids, each with 23 chromosomes (single chromosome with haploid DNA). questions.text: The human ovum is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Motile eggs possess the ability to move independently, which is not characteristic of human eggs. In humans, the ovum is not capable of self-propulsion. Instead, it is transported through the female reproductive tract by ciliary action. Hence, this option is incorrect.

Non-motile eggs do not have the ability to move on their own. The human ovum is classified as non-motile because it relies on the movement of the surrounding fluids and cilia for transportation within the female reproductive system. This option accurately describes the human ovum, making it the correct answer.
Flagellated eggs possess flagella that allow them to swim. This is not applicable to human eggs, as they lack flagella. Human eggs do not exhibit this characteristic, making this option incorrect.

Ciliated eggs have tiny hair-like structures (cilia) that aid in movement. Although the human ovum is transported by cilia in the reproductive tract, it itself does not have cilia. Therefore, this option is also incorrect.

questions.explanation: The ovum is large, non-motile, moved by cilia and muscular contractions of fallopian tube.

questions.text: The function of copper ions in copper releasing IUDs is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Copper ions in copper-releasing IUDs primarily act locally in the uterus to prevent fertilization and pregnancy. They do not inhibit gametogenesis, which is the formation of gametes (sperm and eggs) in the gonads (testes and ovaries).

options.text: They make uterus suitable for implantation
options.explanation: Copper ions in IUDs do not make the uterus more suitable for implantation. In fact, they can have the opposite effect by causing localized inflammation in the uterine lining, making it less conducive to implantation.

Copper-releasing IUDs primarily work by inhibiting fertilization, not ovulation. Unlike hormonal contraceptives, which can prevent ovulation, copper IUDs do not have a significant effect on the release of eggs from the ovaries.

options.text: They suppress sperm motility and fertilising capacity of sperms
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. Copper ions released by copper IUDs have a spermicidal effect. They impair sperm motility (the ability of sperm to swim) and reduce the fertilizing capacity of sperm. This helps prevent sperm from reaching and fertilizing an egg.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is: d. They suppress sperm motility and fertilizing capacity of sperms. Let's discuss each option and explain why the correct answer is option d: a. They do not inhibit gametogenesis, which is the formation of gametes (sperm and eggs) in the gonads (testes and ovaries). b. In fact, they can have the opposite effect by causing localized inflammation in the uterine lining, making it less conducive to implantation. c. They inhibit ovulation: Copper-releasing IUDs primarily work by inhibiting fertilization, not ovulation. Unlike hormonal contraceptives, which can prevent ovulation, copper IUDs do not have a significant effect on the release of eggs from the ovaries. d. They suppress sperm motility and fertilizing capacity of sperms: This is the correct answer. Copper ions released by copper IUDs have a spermicidal effect. They impair sperm motility (the ability of sperm to swim) and reduce the fertilizing capacity of sperm. This helps prevent sperm from reaching and fertilizing an egg. Summary: The function of copper ions in copper-releasing IUDs is to suppress sperm motility and reduce the fertilizing capacity of sperm, primarily by causing a localized inflammatory response in the uterus. This makes it less likely for sperm to successfully fertilize an egg, thus acting as a highly effective method of contraception.

questions.text: Which of the following is hormone­releasing IUD?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: LNG-20 (Levonorgestrel 20) is a hormone-releasing intrauterine device (IUD). It contains the hormone levonorgestrel, which is released slowly over time. This hormone has contraceptive effects, primarily by thickening cervical mucus to prevent sperm from reaching the egg, inhibiting ovulation, and altering the uterine lining to make it less receptive to implantation. It is a highly effective form of long-acting contraception.

Multiload 375 is a copper-containing IUD (Intrauterine Device), not a hormone-releasing IUD. It does not release hormones but instead uses copper to create a spermicidal effect, preventing fertilization.

The Lippes loop is an older form of intrauterine contraception that doesn't release hormones. It's a mechanical IUD made of plastic or stainless steel, and it primarily works by interfering with sperm movement and possibly affecting fertilization. It is not a hormone-releasing IUD
Cu7 is another type of copper-containing IUD, which means it doesn't release hormones either. It relies on the copper ions it releases to disrupt sperm function and prevent fertilization.

questions.text: Which of the following is incorrect regarding vasectomy?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: No sperm occurs in seminal fluid
options.explanation: This statement is correct. After a vasectomy, sperm is absent in the seminal fluid. This is because the vas deferens, which transport sperm from the testicles to the urethra, are cut or sealed, preventing sperm from mixing with the seminal fluid during ejaculation.

This statement is incorrect. The epididymis remains unaffected by a vasectomy. Sperm continue to be produced and stored in the epididymis; however, they cannot travel beyond this point due to the cutting or sealing of the vas deferens.

options.text: Vasa deferentia is cut and tied
options.explanation: This statement is correct. A vasectomy involves cutting, tying, or sealing the vasa deferentia to block sperm from reaching the urethra and being ejaculated.
This option is incorrect because one of the statements about sperm in the epididymis is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is: b. No sperm occurs in epididymis Let's go through each option and explain why the correct answer is b: a. No sperm occurs in seminal fluid: This statement is correct. After a vasectomy, sperm is no longer present in the seminal fluid (ejaculate). The procedure involves cutting or sealing the vas deferens, which are the tubes that carry sperm from the testicles to the urethra. As a result, sperm cannot mix with the seminal fluid during ejaculation. b. No sperm occurs in epididymis: This statement is incorrect. The epididymis is a structure located on the posterior aspect of each testicle and serves as a storage and maturation site for sperm. A vasectomy does not affect the presence of sperm in the epididymis. It only prevents sperm from reaching the ejaculate by blocking the vas deferens. c. Vasa deferentia is cut and tied: This statement is correct. In a vasectomy, the vasa deferentia, which are the tubes that carry sperm from the testicles to the urethra, are cut, tied, or sealed to prevent the release of sperm during ejaculation. Summary: Vasectomy involves cutting or sealing the vasa deferentia, preventing sperm from being ejaculated in the seminal fluid. However, sperm remains in the epididymis, and this is the incorrect statement.

questions.text: Embryo with more than 16 blastomeres formed due to in vitro fertilisation is transferred into:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The embryo is transferred to the uterus as it is the natural site for implantation and further development post-fertilization.
Embryos are not typically transferred to the Fallopian tube after IVF, as it is not suitable for embryos with more than 16 blastomeres.
The fimbriae are involved in capturing the oocyte from the ovary, not in receiving embryos post-IVF.
The cervix is the lower part of the uterus and not the appropriate site for embryo implantation.

questions.explanation: Embryo with more than 16 blastomeres formed due to in vitro fertilisation is transferred into uterus (intra­uterine transfer, IUT).

questions.text: Which of the following approaches does not give the defined action of contraceptive?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Vasectomy is a permanent male sterilization method that involves cutting or sealing the vas deferens to prevent sperm from entering the ejaculate.

Condoms are a barrier method of contraception that physically prevent sperm from entering the uterus, thereby preventing fertilization. This is not an approach that fails to give the defined action of contraceptive.

The withdrawal method, also known as coitus interruptus, involves removing the penis from the vagina before ejaculation. It is not a reliable contraceptive method as pre-ejaculate fluid can contain sperm, making it the correct choice for a method that does not reliably give the defined action of contraception.
Spermicides are chemical agents that deactivate or kill sperm, preventing them from fertilizing an egg. They are designed to provide contraceptive action.

questions.explanation: Vasectomy is a sterilisation technique for the males in which a small part of the vas deferens is removed or tied up through a small cut on the scrotum to prevent passage of sperms. Spermatogenesis is the series of cell division in the testis that results in the production of spermatozoa or sperms.

questions.text: In the context of amniocentesis, which of the following statements is incorrect?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: It can be used for detection of Down’s syndrome
options.explanation: Amniocentesis is a prenatal diagnostic procedure that can detect chromosomal abnormalities, such as Down's syndrome, by analyzing fetal chromosomes in the amniotic fluid.

options.text: It can be used for detection of cleft palate
options.explanation: Amniocentesis is not used to detect physical abnormalities such as cleft palate. It is primarily used for detecting genetic or chromosomal disorders.

options.text: It is usually done when a woman is between 14-16 weeks pregnant
options.explanation: While slightly off, this statement is generally acceptable as amniocentesis is usually performed between 15 and 20 weeks of pregnancy.

options.text: It is used for prenatal sex determination
options.explanation: Amniocentesis can be used to determine the sex of the fetus, in addition to detecting genetic and chromosomal abnormalities.

questions.explanation: The incorrect statement regarding amniocentesis is:b. It can be used for detection of cleft palate.Now, let's explain each option and why the correct answer is b:a. It can be used for detection of Down's syndrome: This statement is correct. Amniocentesis is a prenatal diagnostic procedure that can be used to detect chromosomal abnormalities, including Down's syndrome, by analyzing the fetal chromosomes in the amniotic fluid.b. It can be used for detection of cleft palate: This statement is incorrect. Amniocentesis is not used to detect physical abnormalities such as cleft palate. It is primarily used for chromosomal analysis and the detection of genetic or chromosomal disorders.c. It is usually done when a woman is between 14-16 weeks pregnant: This statement is generally correct. Amniocentesis is typically performed between 15 and 20 weeks of pregnancy, which is the second trimester.d. It is used for prenatal sex determination: This statement is correct. Amniocentesis can be used to determine the sex of the fetus, along with its potential for detecting genetic and chromosomal abnormalities.Summary:Amniocentesis is a prenatal diagnostic procedure commonly performed in the second trimester of pregnancy (between 15 and 20 weeks). It is primarily used for the detection of chromosomal abnormalities and genetic disorders. It can also be used for prenatal sex determination. However, it is not a method for detecting physical abnormalities such as cleft palate.

questions.text: A childless couple can be assisted to have a child through a technique called GIFT. The full form of this technique is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the term 'Internal Fertilisation' does not accurately describe the GIFT procedure, which does not involve fertilisation outside the body.
This option is incorrect as 'Germ cell' is not the term used in the context of this technique, and 'Internal Fallopian Transfer' is not correctly phrased for GIFT.
This is the correct full form of GIFT. The technique involves transferring both the egg and sperm into the fallopian tube, allowing natural fertilisation to occur.
This option is incorrect as the correct full form of GIFT is listed among the options.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is:c. Gamete Intra Fallopian TransferNow, let's explain each option and why the correct answer is c:a. Gamete Internal Fertilization and Transfer: This option does not represent the correct full form of the technique. While it contains some relevant terms like "gamete" and "transfer," it is not the proper terminology for the GIFT technique.b. Germ cell Internal Fallopian Transfer: This option is not the correct full form either. "Germ cell" is not typically used to describe this technique, and "Internal Fallopian Transfer" is not the appropriate wording.c. Gamete Intra Fallopian Transfer: This is the correct full form of the technique. GIFT stands for Gamete Intra Fallopian Transfer. In GIFT, both the egg and sperm are collected and then immediately transferred into the fallopian tube, where fertilization can occur naturally.Summary:The full form of the technique that can assist a childless couple in having a child is "Gamete Intra Fallopian Transfer" (GIFT). This technique involves transferring both the egg and sperm into the fallopian tube for fertilization.

questions.text: Which of the following viruses is not transferred through semen of an infected male?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Chikungunya is primarily transmitted to humans through the bite of infected Aedes mosquitoes, such as Aedes aegypti and Aedes albopictus. While the virus can be present in the blood and other bodily fluids, sexual transmission of the Chikungunya virus through semen is considered extremely rare.

The Ebola virus can be found in various body fluids, including semen. It is documented that sexual transmission is possible, as men can continue to shed the virus in their semen even after recovery from Ebola.
The Hepatitis B virus is present in semen and other bodily fluids. Sexual transmission is a common mode of transmission for Hepatitis B, easily spread through sexual contact with an infected partner.

HIV is found in high concentrations in blood, semen, vaginal fluids, rectal fluids, and breast milk. Sexual transmission is a well-established mode of HIV transmission, making it easily spread through sexual contact with an infected partner.

questions.explanation: Correct answer isa. Chikungunya virusHere's an explanation of each option:a. Chikungunya virus: Chikungunya is primarily transmitted to humans through the bite of infected Aedes mosquitoes, particularly Aedes aegypti and Aedes albopictus. While the virus can be found in the blood and other bodily fluids of infected individuals, sexual transmission of Chikungunya virus through semen is considered rare.b. Ebola virus: The Ebola virus can be present in various body fluids, including semen, and it is known to be transmissible through sexual contact. After recovery from Ebola, men can continue to shed the virus in their semen for an extended period, and sexual transmission has been documented in some cases.c. Hepatitis B virus: The Hepatitis B virus can be present in semen and other bodily fluids. Sexual transmission is one of the common modes of transmission for Hepatitis B, and it can readily be spread through sexual contact with an infected partner.d. Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV): HIV is a virus that can be found in high concentrations in blood, semen, vaginal fluids, rectal fluids, and breast milk. Sexual transmission is a well-established mode of HIV transmission, and it can be easily transmitted through sexual contact with an infected partner.Summary:Chikungunya virus (option a) is not typically transmitted through semen, while the other options (Ebola virus, Hepatitis B virus, and Human immunodeficiency virus) can be transmitted through sexual contact, with a significant risk associated with the latter two. The correct answer is (a) Chikungunya virus.

questions.text: Tubectomy is a method of sterilization in which:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Small part of the Fallopian tube is removed or tied up
options.explanation: Tubectomy is a surgical procedure for female sterilization where the Fallopian tubes are cut, tied, or otherwise blocked. This prevents eggs from traveling from the ovaries to the uterus, making pregnancy impossible. It is a permanent form of contraception.

Oophorectomy is the surgical removal of one or both ovaries. It is not a method of sterilization but may be performed for other medical reasons, such as ovarian cancer or other conditions affecting the ovaries.

options.text: Small part of vas deferens is removed or tied up
options.explanation: This procedure is known as a vasectomy and pertains to male sterilization. It involves cutting or sealing the vas deferens to prevent sperm from entering the ejaculate. It is not related to female sterilization.

Hysterectomy is the surgical removal of the uterus. It is not a sterilization technique, as it is performed for different medical conditions such as fibroids, endometriosis, or cancer, and results in the inability to carry a pregnancy.

questions.explanation: The correct option is: a. Small part of the Fallopian tube is removed or tied up Here's an explanation of each option:a. Small part of the Fallopian tube is removed or tied up: Tubectomy, also known as female sterilization, involves blocking or sealing a woman's Fallopian tubes. This procedure can be done by removing a small section of the tubes or by tying them off, which prevents the eggs from moving from the ovaries to the uterus. It's a permanent method of contraception. b. Ovaries are removed surgically: Surgical removal of the ovaries is called oophorectomy and is not the same as tubectomy. Oophorectomy may be performed for various medical reasons, but it is not a method of sterilization. . Small part of vas deferens is removed or tied up: This procedure is known as a vasectomy and is a form of male sterilization. It involves cutting or blocking the vas deferens, the tubes that carry sperm from the testes to the urethra. It's a permanent method of contraception for men. d. Uterus is removed surgically: Surgical removal of the uterus is called a hysterectomy and is not a method of sterilization. Hysterectomy is typically performed for various medical reasons, such as treating uterine conditions like fibroids or addressing certain gynecological cancers. Summary: Tubectomy (option a) is a method of sterilization for women, where a small part of the Fallopian tube is removed or tied up to prevent the eggs from reaching the uterus, thus making pregnancy impossible. It is a permanent method of contraception. The other options (b, c, and d) refer to different surgical procedures but are not methods of sterilization through blocking or removing the Fallopian tubes.

questions.text: Assisted reproductive technology, IVF involves transfer of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect as IVF involves fertilization outside the body. The ovum is not transferred into the Fallopian tube; rather, the fertilized embryo is transferred into the uterus.
This option is incorrect for standard IVF procedures. The zygote is typically transferred into the uterus, not the Fallopian tube, to bypass potential tubal issues.
This is correct. In IVF, after fertilization occurs in the lab, the resulting zygote develops into an embryo, which is then transferred into the woman's uterus to establish a pregnancy.

options.text: Embryo with 16 blastomeres into the Fallopian tube
options.explanation: This is incorrect. In IVF, embryos are transferred into the uterus rather than the Fallopian tube, and the specific number of blastomeres is less relevant than the developmental stage.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is:c. Zygote into the uterusHere's an explanation of each option:a. Ovum into the Fallopian tube:This is not part of the IVF process. In IVF, the fertilization process takes place outside the body, typically in a laboratory setting, and the resulting embryo is then transferred into the uterus.b. Zygote into the Fallopian tube:This is not the standard IVF procedure. In traditional IVF, the fertilized egg (zygote) is transferred into the uterus, not the Fallopian tube. The purpose of IVF is to bypass potential issues related to the Fallopian tubes and allow fertilization to occur in vitro (outside the body).c. Zygote into the uterus:This is the correct procedure for IVF. After fertilization, the resulting zygote develops into an embryo, and the embryo is then transferred into the woman's uterus to establish a pregnancy.d. Embryo with 16 blastomeres into the Fallopian tube:This is not a part of the IVF process. In IVF, embryos are typically transferred directly into the uterus. The number of blastomeres is not as critical as the developmental stage when determining embryo transfer.Summary:In IVF, the transfer of a zygote (resulting from the fertilization of an egg and sperm) into the woman's uterus is a crucial step in the process. This allows for the establishment of a pregnancy, which is the primary goal of IVF. The correct answer is option c.

questions.text: Artificial insemination means:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: This method involves introducing donor sperm into the woman's vagina, with the expectation that the sperm will travel through the cervix and into the uterus to potentially fertilize an egg. It is one form of artificial insemination
options.explanation: Hence the correct answer is option A. Artificial insemination means the Artificial introduction of sperms of a healthy donor into the vagina.

options.text: Introduction of sperms of a healthy donor directly into the ovary
options.explanation: The introduction of sperm of a healthy donor into the vagina is called artificial insemination. It is an assisted reproductive technology (ART) that can help couples with difficulty conceiving naturally. In artificial insemination, sperm is collected from a donor and then placed directly into the woman's vagina. This can be done during the woman's most fertile time of the month.

options.text: Transfer of sperms of a healthy donor to a test tube containing ova
options.explanation: The transfer of sperm of husband to a test tube containing ova is called intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI). It is an assisted reproductive technology (ART) that can help couples with difficulty conceiving naturally. In ICSI, a sperm is injected directly into an egg in a laboratory dish. This is done for men who have very low sperm counts or who have sperm that are not motile (not able to swim).
Option A is correct

questions.explanation: Hence the correct answer is option A. This method involves introducing donor sperm into the woman's vagina, with the expectation that the sperm will travel through the cervix and into the uterus to potentially fertilize an egg. It is one form of artificial insemination.

questions.text: The stage transferred into the uterus after induced fertilization of ovum in the laboratory is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the female reproductive cell that is fertilized by a sperm to form a zygote. Ova are usually not transferred into the uterus.

After fertilization, when the sperm has successfully fused with the egg, it forms a zygote. This zygote then begins to divide and develop into an embryo. In the context of in vitro fertilization (IVF) or other assisted reproductive techniques, it is the embryo that is usually transferred into the uterus to facilitate pregnancy.

The morula is a stage that follows the zygote. It is a ball of cells resulting from the rapid division of the zygote. Eventually, the morula will develop into a blastocyst, which is the stage that is usually transferred into the uterus during IVF.

The zygote is the product of the fusion of a sperm cell and an egg cell. It is the first stage of development after fertilization, but it is not typically transferred into the uterus. Instead, it undergoes further development into the morula and eventually the blastocyst or embryo.

questions.explanation: The stage transferred into the uterus after induced fertilization of an ovum in the laboratory is typically the embryo. Let's go through each option and provide some explanation:a. Ova (egg): This is the female reproductive cell that is fertilized by a sperm to form a zygote. Ova are usually not transferred into the uterus.b. Embryo: After fertilization, when the sperm has successfully fused with the egg, it forms a zygote. This zygote then begins to divide and develop into an embryo. In the context of in vitro fertilization (IVF) or other assisted reproductive techniques, it is the embryo that is usually transferred into the uterus to facilitate pregnancy.c. Morula: The morula is a stage that follows the zygote. It is a ball of cells resulting from the rapid division of the zygote. Eventually, the morula will develop into a blastocyst, which is the stage that is usually transferred into the uterus during IVF.d. Zygote: The zygote is the product of the fusion of a sperm cell and an egg cell. It is the first stage of development after fertilization, but it is not typically transferred into the uterus. Instead, it undergoes further development into the morula and eventually the blastocyst or embryo.Correct Answer: The correct answer is b. Embryo. After induced fertilization in the laboratory, the resulting zygote develops into an embryo, and it is the embryo that is transferred into the uterus for implantation during assisted reproductive techniques like IVF.Summary: The stage transferred into the uterus after induced fertilization is the embryo. While the zygote is the immediate product of fertilization, it undergoes further development into the morula and then the blastocyst, which is what is typically transferred into the uterus during assisted reproductive procedures to establish pregnancy.

questions.text: One of the following is not a method of contraception. Which one?

options.explanation: Condoms are a widely used barrier method of contraception that prevents sperm from entering the uterus.

options.text: Pills of a combination of oxytocin and vasopressin
options.explanation: While oxytocin and vasopressin are hormones, they are not used in contraceptive methods. Oxytocin is associated with childbirth and vasopressin with water retention.

questions.explanation: Oxytocin is a birth hormone and vasopressin (anti­diuretic hormone) reabsorbs water from the renal tubules to conserve water in the body. They have no role in contraception.

questions.text: The test­ tube baby programme employs which one of the following techniques?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It involves the direct injection of a single sperm into an egg to facilitate fertilization. ICSI is particularly useful in cases of male infertility, where there may be issues with the sperm's ability to penetrate the egg. While ICSI is a component of IVF, it is not the primary technique used in "test-tube baby" programs.

IUI is a distinct procedure from IVF. It entails the placement of prepared sperm directly into a woman's uterus to increase the chances of fertilization. However, in IUI, fertilization occurs within the woman's body, not in a laboratory dish. It is typically used in cases where the sperm may need assistance in reaching the egg but is not the primary technique used in "test-tube baby" programs.

ZIFT is another assisted reproductive technique similar to IVF. In ZIFT, fertilization takes place outside the body in a laboratory dish, much like in IVF. However, the key difference is that, with ZIFT, the fertilized zygote is transferred into the woman's fallopian tube instead of her uterus. ZIFT is less commonly used than standard IVF.

All are methods. While IVF is the primary technique used in "test-tube baby" programs, the other methods (ICSI, IUI, and ZIFT) may also be incorporated depending on the specific needs and circumstances of the individuals or couples seeking assistance with conception.

questions.explanation: The "test-tube baby" program employs the technique of in vitro fertilization (IVF). In IVF, the fertilization of an egg (ovum) by sperm occurs outside the body, typically in a laboratory dish, and the resulting embryo is then transferred into the woman's uterus. This technique is used to assist individuals or couples who have difficulty conceiving naturally and is a common method of assisted reproductive technology (ART).Let's explain the provided options in more detail:a. Intra-cytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI): ICSI is a method used within the IVF process. It involves the direct injection of a single sperm into an egg to facilitate fertilization. ICSI is particularly useful in cases of male infertility, where there may be issues with the sperm's ability to penetrate the egg. While ICSI is a component of IVF, it is not the primary technique used in "test-tube baby" programs.b. Intra-uterine insemination (IUI): IUI is a distinct procedure from IVF. It entails the placement of prepared sperm directly into a woman's uterus to increase the chances of fertilization. However, in IUI, fertilization occurs within the woman's body, not in a laboratory dish. It is typically used in cases where the sperm may need assistance in reaching the egg but is not the primary technique used in "test-tube baby" programs.c. Zygote intra-Fallopian transfer (ZIFT): ZIFT is another assisted reproductive technique similar to IVF. In ZIFT, fertilization takes place outside the body in a laboratory dish, much like in IVF. However, the key difference is that, with ZIFT, the fertilized zygote is transferred into the woman's fallopian tube instead of her uterus. ZIFT is less commonly used than standard IVF.Correct Answer: The correct answer is d. All are methods. While IVF is the primary technique used in "test-tube baby" programs, the other methods (ICSI, IUI, and ZIFT) may also be incorporated depending on the specific needs and circumstances of the individuals or couples seeking assistance with conception.Summary: The primary technique employed in "test-tube baby" programs is in vitro fertilization (IVF). ICSI, IUI, and ZIFT are complementary methods that may be used in conjunction with IVF or in specific cases of infertility. The choice of method depends on the individual's or couple's fertility issues and the recommendations of their healthcare providers.

questions.text: Which one of the following is the most widely accepted method of contraception in India at present?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: IUDs are indeed a popular and effective form of contraception in India. They are long-term and reversible, making them a choice for many women who want to delay or space pregnancies.
As mentioned earlier, tubectomy is also widely accepted in India, particularly as a permanent method of contraception. Many women opt for tubectomy as a means of family planning.

Diaphragms are barrier methods of contraception. They are soft, shallow cups made of silicone or latex that are inserted into the vagina to cover the cervix before sexual intercourse. Diaphragms, when used correctly with spermicide, can be effective at preventing pregnancy. However, they are less commonly used in India compared to other methods like IUDs and tubectomy. They require proper fitting, placement, and the use of spermicide, which may not always be practical or readily available in certain regions.

This option indicates that both IUDs and Tubectomy are widely accepted methods of contraception in India. IUDs provide a reversible, long-term option, while Tubectomy is a permanent method of contraception. The choice between these two methods often depends on individual preferences, family planning goals, and other factors.

questions.text: In vitro fertilisation is a technique that involves transfer of which one of the following into theFallopian tube?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Embryo only, up to 8 cell stage
options.explanation: This option is correct because during in vitro fertilisation, the embryo is typically transferred to the Fallopian tube at the 8 cell stage, which is optimal for implantation.

options.text: Either zygote or early embryo up to 8 cell stage

questions.explanation: In vitro fertilisation (IVF) is a process that involves fertilising an egg outside the body and then transferring the resulting embryo into the Fallopian tube. The most optimal stage for this transfer is typically at the 8 cell stage, as it has the highest chances of successful implantation. questions.text: The permissible use of the technique amnio­-centesis is for:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Detecting sex of the unborn foetus
options.explanation: It is incorrect. It is to detect genetic abnormalities.
It is incorrect. It is to detect genetic abnormalities.

options.text: Transfer of embryo into the uterus of a surrogate mother

Amniocentesis is a withdrawal of a sample of the fluid (amniotic fluid) surrounding a fetus in the uterus by piercing the amniotic sac through the abdominal wall, under direct ultrasound guidance. As the amniotic fluid contains cells from the fetus, cell cultures enable chromosome patterns to be studied so that prenatal diagnosis of chromosomal abnormalities can be made

questions.explanation: Amniocentesis is a withdrawal of a sample of the fluid (amniotic fluid) surrounding a fetus in the uterus by piercing the amniotic sac through the abdominal wall, under direct ultrasound guidance. As the amniotic fluid contains cells from the fetus, cell cultures enable chromosome patterns to be studied so that prenatal diagnosis of chromosomal abnormalities can be made. Certain metabolic errors and other abnormalities, such as spina bifida, can also be diagnosed prenatally from analysis of the cells or of the fluid. As this technique also helps in the detection of the sex of the unborn fetus, it has been banned in order to legally check increasing female foeticides.
